design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate large 2D platforming levels with task-based unlock mechanics (pass through hoops, activate disappearing platforms, ride roller coasters) to reveal exit warps; defeat enemies via limited star projectiles or aerial drill attacks
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Instantaneous spike hazards requiring precise platforming execution combined with task completion requirements that gate progression
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Aero as a circus acrobat bat hero rescuing kidnapped performers and stopping an industrialist villain's sabotage scheme
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberate use of 'awkwardly positioned spikes' as core level design philosophy; task-gating levels rather than straightforward traversal; bonus levels as score-based unlocks (pool diving on Genesis, Mode 7 overworld flight on SNES) create alternate reward pathways

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Score accumulation unlocks bonus levels, providing optional challenge escalation; aerial drill attack positioning requires precision execution

progression.upgrade_system: Limited star ammunition for ranged attacks; aerial drill attack as default melee option
progression.unlock_mechanism: Task completion within levels reveals exit warps; score thresholds unlock bonus levels
replayability_hooks: Bonus level access tied to point accumulation encourages score optimization
Large level design with multiple task pathways suggests exploration for alternate solutions
Distinction between Genesis pool-diving and SNES Mode 7 bonus mechanics provides platform-specific variety

world_building_techniques: Character background: Edgar Ektor's origin (banned circus attendee turned industrialist) provides revenge motivation
Relationship stakes: Aeriel (Aero's girlfriend) kidnapped as personal victim among performers
Thematic setting: World of Amusement Circus and Funpark as both heritage site and sabotage target
Antagonist coalition: Psycho Circus gang as villain ensemble suggesting corrupted carnival theme

faction_indicators: Circus performers as rescue targets (allied faction)
Edgar Ektor's Psycho Circus gang (antagonist faction)
Zero the Kamikaze Squirrel as secondary villain (spinoff-connected antagonist)
danger_signals: Instantaneous spike damage as environmental threat
Limited ammunition (star projectiles) as resource scarcity signal
Kidnapped performers as captive population indicator
Sabotage of funpark infrastructure as economic/cultural damage
environmental_storytelling: Funpark setting degradation through sabotage narrative
Circus performance context explains acrobatic movement mechanics
20-year time gap between Edgar's banishment and industrialist return suggests long-term threat buildup

sources_used: wikipedia: Aero the Acro-Bat gameplay section (level structure, tasks, spikes, attack mechanics, bonus levels)
wikipedia: Plot section (character relationships, villain motivation, kidnapping stakes)
wikipedia: Development section (David Siller character intent, Mappy inspiration, Sunsoft context)
